2 min read
Just now

--

We knew it does. But the naysayers didn’t.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

From Financial TimesDavid Sacks posted this on X.
A new study of 21,559 firms in the U.S. finds that “companies that adopt AI tend to grow faster following adoption”.
“Firms making the largest AI investments grow employment by roughly 10% following adoption, while low-intensity adopters see no statistically significant change.”
“Entry-level headcount rises 12% for high-intensity adopters.”
“Gains emerge gradually and are broad across roles, including engineering, sales, administration, and customer service.”
“The results counter predictions that AI adoption will lead to broad job loss.”
The study is based on observed AI spending from Ramp card and bill pay data linked to Revelio Labs workforce records.
I still think we’ll see employment go down as AI gets too good. (But IMO we’ll see a net growth in prosperity as jobs are spread by a legislated 4 day week etc).
But whatever the case, this study of over 21K companies proves there’s statistically significant ROI to be found in AI use.
Yes, the red group were slightly increasing in the first 10 months — showing that smart companies do better anyway.
But that growth on average was nowhere near as much as the next 10 months. Not to mentioned the 10 months after that.
Everyone.
It’s true.
And it’s also true that naysayers are usually wrong.
In this case most of us knew it’s gotta be helping.
But it’s hard to measure ROI.
This is a great unbiased way to do it.
Which companies grew?
The AI believers or the doubters?
